tasks.name: 02 — Infra Build (Keeper) — Hardened checklist
tasks.text_content:
Outcome

A DigitalOcean Ubuntu 24.04 LTS droplet with Caddy as public reverse proxy, automatic TLS, SSH locked to keys-only, root login disabled, fail2ban monitoring SSH + Caddy logs, and UFW configured. This is the substrate for the rest of the infra stack.

Hardening checklist

1. Provision droplet
- Ubuntu 24.04 LTS
- 8 vCPU / 16 GB / NVMe
- Region chosen for latency
- DNS A records pointed at droplet IP

2. First-login hygiene
- apt update && apt -y full-upgrade && apt -y autoremove
- install ufw, fail2ban, curl, ca-certificates, unattended-upgrades
- enable unattended-upgrades
- hostnamectl set-hostname twlv20-prod
- timedatectl set-timezone UTC

3. Create sudo users
- create keeper and jon
- add both to sudo
- install public keys in ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
- verify both can SSH in before locking down sshd

4. Harden sshd
- use /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/99-hardening.conf
- PermitRootLogin no
- PasswordAuthentication no
- ChallengeResponseAuthentication no
- KbdInteractiveAuthentication no
- PubkeyAuthentication yes
- MaxAuthTries 3
- LoginGraceTime 20
- AllowUsers keeper jon
- validate with sshd -t
- restart ssh and re-verify access from a second terminal

5. UFW
- default deny incoming
- default allow outgoing
- limit 22/tcp
- allow 80/tcp
- allow 443/tcp
- enable and confirm status verbose

6. Install Caddy
- add official Cloudsmith repo
- apt install caddy
- confirm caddy service active and caddy version

7. Caddy baseline
- configure email and JSON logs
- define timeouts
- create host blocks for paperclip, claw, infisical
- reverse proxy each to its local upstream port
- validate config and reload
- confirm TLS certificate issuance

8. fail2ban
- ssh jail enabled and active
- caddy-status filter for repeated 401/403/404
- caddy-status jail active

9. Final lockdown
- disable root password login
- enable ufw, fail2ban, caddy, unattended-upgrades

Acceptance criteria
- root SSH denied
- keeper and jon SSH with key only
- sshd hardening file contains PermitRootLogin no and PasswordAuthentication no
- UFW shows 22 LIMIT, 80 ALLOW, 443 ALLOW, default deny incoming
- caddy active
- HTTPS handshake works for at least one subdomain
- sshd fail2ban jail active
- caddy-status fail2ban jail active
- unattended-upgrades dry-run shows daemon will run

Hazards
- do not lock down sshd from the only open session
- keep a second terminal open while changing sshd or final lockdown
- do not close port 80 or Caddy ACME will fail
- do not edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config directly; use the drop-in

Review gate
- Jon logs in as jon
- Jon runs acceptance checks
- Jon deliberately confirms root SSH and password-only SSH fail
- Keeper signs off after both failures

Owner
- Keeper

tasks.name: GitHub repo scaffold + CI + deploy hook (`twlv20-ai-skills`)
tasks.text_content:
Outcome

The private repo twlv20-ai-skills is scaffolded with the per-tenant skill folder layout, baseline CI on every PR (lint + unit tests against the scaffold), and a deploy workflow that on merge to main SSHes to the droplet, pulls latest, and restarts the runtime via systemd. Branch protection is on. A no-op end-to-end deploy has fired successfully against the live droplet.

Repo layout

twlv20-ai-skills/
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       ├── ci.yml              # lint + test on PR
│       └── deploy.yml          # deploy on merge to main
├── infra/
│   ├── schema.sql              # Plinko-authored, Keeper applies (Task 3)
│   ├── migrations/             # versioned forward migrations
│   └── tests/
│       └── rls_isolation.sql   # the RLS pairwise test
├── runtime/
│   ├── package.json
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── index.ts            # OpenClaw entry; agent runtime bootstrap
│   │   ├── intake/             # Slack, WhatsApp, voice, Gmail intake
│   │   ├── integrations/       # ghl.ts, smartlead.ts, fbads.ts, vercel.ts
│   │   ├── runlog/             # Run-Log writer
│   │   └── tests/
│   └── tsconfig.json
├── skills/
│   ├── global/
│   │   ├── ghl-health-check/
│   │   ├── site-build-chain/
│   │   └── clickup-infra-pass/
│   ├── pure-peptide/
│   ├── ajiri/
│   ├── sru/
│   └── twlv20-internal/
│       └── (each skill folder: skill.yaml, prompt.md, tests/)
├── docs/
│   ├── ARCHITECTURE.md         # link/excerpt of the v6.0 contract
│   └── RUNBOOKS/
├── README.md
└── .gitignore

The runtime/ Node project is a thin host that loads skills from skills/ based on tenant + workflow. Plinko owns the runtime code; Keeper owns scaffolding it well so deploys are trivial.

Step-by-step

1. Initialize the repo

In Twlv20's GitHub org (List 01 → "GitHub — org access + private repo"):

git clone git@github.com:twlv20/twlv20-ai-skills.git
cd twlv20-ai-skills
mkdir -p .github/workflows infra/migrations infra/tests runtime/src/{intake,integrations,runlog,tests} skills/{global,pure-peptide,ajiri,sru,twlv20-internal} docs/RUNBOOKS
touch infra/schema.sql infra/tests/rls_isolation.sql

2. README.md

Lay out the repo's purpose, the skill convention, and the deploy story. Use the layout above as the "Structure" section. Keep the skill folder spec short:

Each skill folder contains:
  skill.yaml   - { name, description, tenant, channels, hitl_required }
  prompt.md    - the system prompt for this skill
  tests/       - unit tests against fixture inputs

3. .gitignore

node_modules/
.env
.env.local
*.dump
*.gpg
.DS_Store
dist/
coverage/

4. CI: .github/workflows/ci.yml

Runs on every PR to main. Currently lints, type-checks, and runs the unit tests against the (mostly empty) scaffold. Plinko's runtime PRs will build on this baseline.

name: CI

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-24.04
    timeout-minutes: 10
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: runtime/package-lock.json
      - name: Install
        working-directory: runtime
        run: npm ci
      - name: Lint
        working-directory: runtime
        run: npm run lint --if-present
      - name: Typecheck
        working-directory: runtime
        run: npm run typecheck --if-present
      - name: Test
        working-directory: runtime
        run: npm test --if-present

  schema-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-24.04
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ci
          POSTGRES_DB: twlv20_ci
        ports: [5432:5432]
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "pg_isready" --health-interval 10s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 5
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Apply schema
        run: |
          sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y postgresql-client-16 postgresql-16-pgvector
          PGPASSWORD=ci psql -h 127.0.0.1 -U postgres -d twlv20_ci -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 -f infra/schema.sql
      - name: Run RLS isolation test
        run: |
          PGPASSWORD=ci psql -h 127.0.0.1 -U postgres -d twlv20_ci -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 -f infra/tests/rls_isolation.sql

The schema-check job is the canary that catches RLS regressions before they hit prod.

5. Deploy workflow: .github/workflows/deploy.yml

Triggers on push to main (post-merge). Uses appleboy/ssh-action — battle-tested, used in many production setups.

name: Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

concurrency:
  group: deploy-prod
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-24.04
    timeout-minutes: 10
    environment:
      name: production
      url: https://claw.<your-domain>
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Deploy via SSH
        uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1
        with:
          host: ${{ secrets.DROPLET_HOST }}
          username: ${{ secrets.DROPLET_DEPLOY_USER }}
          key: ${{ secrets.DROPLET_DEPLOY_KEY }}
          port: 22
          command_timeout: 5m
          script: |
            set -euo pipefail
            cd /opt/twlv20-ai-skills
            git fetch --all --prune
            git reset --hard origin/main
            cd runtime
            npm ci --omit=dev
            npm run build
            sudo systemctl restart twlv20-runtime.service
            sudo systemctl is-active twlv20-runtime.service

6. Provision the deploy user + key

On the droplet:

sudo adduser --disabled-password --gecos '' twlv20deploy
sudo install -d -o twlv20deploy -g twlv20deploy -m 700 /home/twlv20deploy/.ssh
sudo install -d -o twlv20deploy -g twlv20deploy /opt/twlv20-ai-skills
sudo chown twlv20deploy:twlv20deploy /opt/twlv20-ai-skills

Generate a deploy key on your laptop (NOT on the droplet — keep the private key off the box):

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f twlv20-deploy -N ''
# Public key:
cat twlv20-deploy.pub
# Private key (paste into GitHub Secret DROPLET_DEPLOY_KEY):
cat twlv20-deploy

Add the public key to /home/twlv20deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys (mode 600, owner twlv20deploy).

Allow twlv20deploy to restart only the runtime service (no other sudo). Edit with visudo:

twlv20deploy ALL=NOPASSWD: /bin/systemctl restart twlv20-runtime.service, /bin/systemctl is-active twlv20-runtime.service

Restrict the deploy user further by limiting their SSH command (optional but recommended):

# In ~twlv20deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys, prefix the key with:
restrict,command="/usr/local/sbin/twlv20-deploy-shell.sh" ssh-ed25519 AAAA...

Where twlv20-deploy-shell.sh whitelists only the deploy commands. For Phase 1, leave this unrestricted — but flag for Phase 2.

7. systemd unit for the runtime

/etc/systemd/system/twlv20-runtime.service (Plinko owns the runtime code; Keeper owns the unit):

[Unit]
Description=Twlv20 OpenClaw runtime
After=network-online.target postgresql.service redis-server.service
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=twlv20deploy
Group=twlv20deploy
WorkingDirectory=/opt/twlv20-ai-skills/runtime
EnvironmentFile=/etc/twlv20-runtime/.env
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node dist/index.js
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal
NoNewPrivileges=true
ProtectSystem=strict
ProtectHome=true
ReadWritePaths=/opt/twlv20-ai-skills /var/log/twlv20-runtime

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

/etc/twlv20-runtime/.env holds the bootstrap secrets (Infisical machine identity client_id + client_secret); the rest is fetched from Infisical at runtime. mode 600.

8. GitHub Actions secrets

In repo Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository secret:

DROPLET_HOST          <droplet IP or hostname>
DROPLET_DEPLOY_USER   twlv20deploy
DROPLET_DEPLOY_KEY    (the private ed25519 key, full contents)

9. Branch protection on main

Settings → Branches → Add rule, target main:

Require a pull request before merging (1 approver minimum).
Require status checks: build, schema-check.
Require branches to be up to date before merging.
Restrict who can push to matching branches: only deploy bots / repo admins.
Disallow force pushes.

10. End-to-end no-op deploy

Open a no-op PR (e.g., a comment in README.md), wait for CI to go green, merge. Watch GitHub Actions; watch journalctl -u twlv20-runtime.service -f on the droplet. Confirm the service restarts and reports active.

Acceptance criteria

Repo exists, scaffolded with the layout above, README.md committed.
.github/workflows/ci.yml runs on PR; both build and schema-check jobs pass.
.github/workflows/deploy.yml triggers on merge to main.
twlv20deploy user on droplet, deploy key authorized, scoped sudo for systemctl restart twlv20-runtime.service.
Branch protection enforced on main: PR + status checks + no force push.
No-op deploy succeeded; systemctl is-active twlv20-runtime.service returns active post-deploy.
Deploy private key never lived on the droplet; only the public key is on the droplet.

Review gate

Jon opens a no-op PR, watches both CI jobs go green, merges, then watches the deploy workflow fire and the systemd service restart cleanly.

Hazards

Don't store the deploy private key on the droplet. It lives in GitHub Secrets and on Keeper's laptop only.
appleboy/ssh-action pinning — pin to a specific tag or SHA, not @v1 floating, to prevent supply-chain risk. Update intentionally.
Don't put real secrets in .env in the repo. The .env on the droplet is built from Infisical at deploy-time; the repo only carries .env.example with placeholders.
Branch protection bypass — keep "include administrators" enabled in branch protection so even an admin push goes through PR. Phase 1 is small enough that this is fine.
schema-check job time — Postgres + pgvector apt install adds ~2 min per CI run. If that becomes painful, build a custom Docker image and pre-install. Phase 1 don't optimize.

Owner

Keeper.

Depends on

GitHub repo provisioned (List 01); droplet hardening (Task 1); Postgres + RLS migration (Tasks 2 + 3 — the schema-check job depends on those).

References

appleboy/ssh-action (GitHub Marketplace)
GitHub Actions: SSH deploy with systemd
GitHub Actions secrets docs
Branch protection rules

The schema-check job is the canary that catches RLS regressions before they hit prod.

5. Deploy workflow: .github/workflows/deploy.yml

Triggers on push to main (post-merge). Uses appleboy/ssh-action — battle-tested, used in many production setups.

name: Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

concurrency:
  group: deploy-prod
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-24.04
    timeout-minutes: 10
    environment:
      name: production
      url: https://claw.<your-domain>
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Deploy via SSH
        uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1
        with:
          host: ${{ secrets.DROPLET_HOST }}
          username: ${{ secrets.DROPLET_DEPLOY_USER }}
          key: ${{ secrets.DROPLET_DEPLOY_KEY }}
          port: 22
          command_timeout: 5m
          script: |
            set -euo pipefail
            cd /opt/twlv20-ai-skills
            git fetch --all --prune
            git reset --hard origin/main
            cd runtime
            npm ci --omit=dev
            npm run build
            sudo systemctl restart twlv20-runtime.service
            sudo systemctl is-active twlv20-runtime.service

6. Provision the deploy user + key

On the droplet:

sudo adduser --disabled-password --gecos '' twlv20deploy
sudo install -d -o twlv20deploy -g twlv20deploy -m 700 /home/twlv20deploy/.ssh
sudo install -d -o twlv20deploy -g twlv20deploy /opt/twlv20-ai-skills
sudo chown twlv20deploy:twlv20deploy /opt/twlv20-ai-skills

Generate a deploy key on your laptop (NOT on the droplet — keep the private key off the box):

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f twlv20-deploy -N ''
# Public key:
cat twlv20-deploy.pub
# Private key (paste into GitHub Secret DROPLET_DEPLOY_KEY):
cat twlv20-deploy

Add the public key to /home/twlv20deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys (mode 600, owner twlv20deploy).

Allow twlv20deploy to restart only the runtime service (no other sudo). Edit with visudo:

twlv20deploy ALL=NOPASSWD: /bin/systemctl restart twlv20-runtime.service, /bin/systemctl is-active twlv20-runtime.service

Restrict the deploy user further by limiting their SSH command (optional but recommended):

# In ~twlv20deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys, prefix the key with:
restrict,command="/usr/local/sbin/twlv20-deploy-shell.sh" ssh-ed25519 AAAA...

Where twlv20-deploy-shell.sh whitelists only the deploy commands. For Phase 1, leave this unrestricted — but flag for Phase 2.

7. systemd unit for the runtime

/etc/systemd/system/twlv20-runtime.service (Plinko owns the runtime code; Keeper owns the unit):

[Unit]
Description=Twlv20 OpenClaw runtime
After=network-online.target postgresql.service redis-server.service
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=twlv20deploy
Group=twlv20deploy
WorkingDirectory=/opt/twlv20-ai-skills/runtime
EnvironmentFile=/etc/twlv20-runtime/.env
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node dist/index.js
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal
NoNewPrivileges=true
ProtectSystem=strict
ProtectHome=true
ReadWritePaths=/opt/twlv20-ai-skills /var/log/twlv20-runtime

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

/etc/twlv20-runtime/.env holds the bootstrap secrets (Infisical machine identity client_id + client_secret); the rest is fetched from Infisical at runtime. mode 600.

8. GitHub Actions secrets

In repo Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository secret:

DROPLET_HOST          <droplet IP or hostname>
DROPLET_DEPLOY_USER   twlv20deploy
DROPLET_DEPLOY_KEY    (the private ed25519 key, full contents)

9. Branch protection on main

Settings → Branches → Add rule, target main:

Require a pull request before merging (1 approver minimum).
Require status checks: build, schema-check.
Require branches to be up to date before merging.
Restrict who can push to matching branches: only deploy bots / repo admins.
Disallow force pushes.

10. End-to-end no-op deploy

Open a no-op PR (e.g., a comment in README.md), wait for CI to go green, merge. Watch GitHub Actions; watch journalctl -u twlv20-runtime.service -f on the droplet. Confirm the service restarts and reports active.

Acceptance criteria

Repo exists, scaffolded with the layout above, README.md committed.
.github/workflows/ci.yml runs on PR; both build and schema-check jobs pass.
.github/workflows/deploy.yml triggers on merge to main.
twlv20deploy user on droplet, deploy key authorized, scoped sudo for systemctl restart twlv20-runtime.service.
Branch protection enforced on main: PR + status checks + no force push.
No-op deploy succeeded; systemctl is-active twlv20-runtime.service returns active post-deploy.
Deploy private key never lived on the droplet; only the public key is on the droplet.

Review gate

Jon opens a no-op PR, watches both CI jobs go green, merges, then watches the deploy workflow fire and the systemd service restart cleanly.

Hazards

Don't store the deploy private key on the droplet. It lives in GitHub Secrets and on Keeper's laptop only.
appleboy/ssh-action pinning — pin to a specific tag or SHA, not @v1 floating, to prevent supply-chain risk. Update intentionally.
Don't put real secrets in .env in the repo. The .env on the droplet is built from Infisical at deploy-time; the repo only carries .env.example with placeholders.
Branch protection bypass — keep "include administrators" enabled in branch protection so even an admin push goes through PR. Phase 1 is small enough that this is fine.
schema-check job time — Postgres + pgvector apt install adds ~2 min per CI run. If that becomes painful, build a custom Docker image and pre-install. Phase 1 don't optimize.

Owner

Keeper.

Depends on

GitHub repo provisioned (List 01); droplet hardening (Task 1); Postgres + RLS migration (Tasks 2 + 3 — the schema-check job depends on those).

References

appleboy/ssh-action (GitHub Marketplace)
GitHub Actions: SSH deploy with systemd
GitHub Actions secrets docs
Branch protection rules
tasks.markdown_description:
## Outcome

The private repo `twlv20-ai-skills` is scaffolded with the per-tenant skill folder layout, baseline CI on every PR (lint + unit tests against the scaffold), and a deploy workflow that on merge to `main` SSHes to the droplet, pulls latest, and restarts the runtime via systemd. Branch protection is on. A no-op end-to-end deploy has fired successfully against the live droplet.

## Repo layout

```php
twlv20-ai-skills/
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       ├── ci.yml              # lint + test on PR
│       └── deploy.yml          # deploy on merge to main
├── infra/
│   ├── schema.sql              # Plinko-authored, Keeper applies (Task 3)
│   ├── migrations/             # versioned forward migrations
│   └── tests/
│       └── rls_isolation.sql   # the RLS pairwise test
├── runtime/
│   ├── package.json
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── index.ts            # OpenClaw entry; agent runtime bootstrap
│   │   ├── intake/             # Slack, WhatsApp, voice, Gmail intake
│   │   ├── integrations/       # ghl.ts, smartlead.ts, fbads.ts, vercel.ts
│   │   ├── runlog/             # Run-Log writer
│   │   └── tests/
│   └── tsconfig.json
├── skills/
│   ├── global/
│   │   ├── ghl-health-check/
│   │   ├── site-build-chain/
│   │   └── clickup-infra-pass/
│   ├── pure-peptide/
│   ├── ajiri/
│   ├── sru/
│   └── twlv20-internal/
│       └── (each skill folder: skill.yaml, prompt.md, tests/)
├── docs/
│   ├── ARCHITECTURE.md         # link/excerpt of the v6.0 contract
│   └── RUNBOOKS/
├── README.md
└── .gitignore
```

The `runtime/` Node project is a thin host that loads skills from `skills/` based on tenant + workflow. Plinko owns the runtime code; Keeper owns scaffolding it well so deploys are trivial.

## Step-by-step

### 1\. Initialize the repo

In Twlv20's GitHub org (List 01 → "GitHub — org access + private repo"):

```bash
git clone git@github.com:twlv20/twlv20-ai-skills.git
cd twlv20-ai-skills
mkdir -p .github/workflows infra/migrations infra/tests runtime/src/{intake,integrations,runlog,tests} skills/{global,pure-peptide,ajiri,sru,twlv20-internal} docs/RUNBOOKS
touch infra/schema.sql infra/tests/rls_isolation.sql
```

### 2\. [README.md](http://README.md)

Lay out the repo's purpose, the skill convention, and the deploy story. Use the layout above as the "Structure" section. Keep the skill folder spec short:

```kotlin
Each skill folder contains:
  skill.yaml   - { name, description, tenant, channels, hitl_required }
  prompt.md    - the system prompt for this skill
  tests/       - unit tests against fixture inputs
```

### 3\. .gitignore

```dpr
node_modules/
.env
.env.local
*.dump
*.gpg
.DS_Store
dist/
coverage/
```

### 4\. CI: `.github/workflows/ci.yml`

Runs on every PR to `main`. Currently lints, type-checks, and runs the unit tests against the (mostly empty) scaffold. Plinko's runtime PRs will build on this baseline.

```yaml
name: CI

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-24.04
    timeout-minutes: 10
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: runtime/package-lock.json
      - name: Install
        working-directory: runtime
        run: npm ci
      - name: Lint
        working-directory: runtime
        run: npm run lint --if-present
      - name: Typecheck
        working-directory: runtime
        run: npm run typecheck --if-present
      - name: Test
        working-directory: runtime
        run: npm test --if-present

  schema-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-24.04
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ci
          POSTGRES_DB: twlv20_ci
        ports: [5432:5432]
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "pg_isready" --health-interval 10s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 5
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Apply schema
        run: |
          sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y postgresql-client-16 postgresql-16-pgvector
          PGPASSWORD=ci psql -h 127.0.0.1 -U postgres -d twlv20_ci -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 -f infra/schema.sql
      - name: Run RLS isolation test
        run: |
          PGPASSWORD=ci psql -h 127.0.0.1 -U postgres -d twlv20_ci -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 -f infra/tests/rls_isolation.sql
```

The `schema-check` job is the canary that catches RLS regressions before they hit prod.

### 5\. Deploy workflow: `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`

Triggers on push to `main` (post-merge). Uses `appleboy/ssh-action` — battle-tested, used in many production setups.

```yaml
name: Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

concurrency:
  group: deploy-prod
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-24.04
    timeout-minutes: 10
    environment:
      name: production
      url: https://claw.<your-domain>
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Deploy via SSH
        uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1
        with:
          host: ${{ secrets.DROPLET_HOST }}
          username: ${{ secrets.DROPLET_DEPLOY_USER }}
          key: ${{ secrets.DROPLET_DEPLOY_KEY }}
          port: 22
          command_timeout: 5m
          script: |
            set -euo pipefail
            cd /opt/twlv20-ai-skills
            git fetch --all --prune
            git reset --hard origin/main
            cd runtime
            npm ci --omit=dev
            npm run build
            sudo systemctl restart twlv20-runtime.service
            sudo systemctl is-active twlv20-runtime.service
```

### 6\. Provision the deploy user + key

On the droplet:

```bash
sudo adduser --disabled-password --gecos '' twlv20deploy
sudo install -d -o twlv20deploy -g twlv20deploy -m 700 /home/twlv20deploy/.ssh
sudo install -d -o twlv20deploy -g twlv20deploy /opt/twlv20-ai-skills
sudo chown twlv20deploy:twlv20deploy /opt/twlv20-ai-skills
```

Generate a deploy key on your laptop (NOT on the droplet — keep the private key off the box):

```bash
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f twlv20-deploy -N ''
# Public key:
cat twlv20-deploy.pub
# Private key (paste into GitHub Secret DROPLET_DEPLOY_KEY):
cat twlv20-deploy
```

Add the public key to `/home/twlv20deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys` (mode 600, owner twlv20deploy).

Allow `twlv20deploy` to restart only the runtime service (no other sudo). Edit with `visudo`:

```javascript
twlv20deploy ALL=NOPASSWD: /bin/systemctl restart twlv20-runtime.service, /bin/systemctl is-active twlv20-runtime.service
```

Restrict the deploy user further by limiting their SSH command (optional but recommended):

```bash
# In ~twlv20deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys, prefix the key with:
restrict,command="/usr/local/sbin/twlv20-deploy-shell.sh" ssh-ed25519 AAAA...
```

Where `twlv20-deploy-shell.sh` whitelists only the deploy commands. For Phase 1, leave this unrestricted — but flag for Phase 2.

### 7\. systemd unit for the runtime

`/etc/systemd/system/twlv20-runtime.service` (Plinko owns the runtime code; Keeper owns the unit):

```plain
[Unit]
Description=Twlv20 OpenClaw runtime
After=network-online.target postgresql.service redis-server.service
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=twlv20deploy
Group=twlv20deploy
WorkingDirectory=/opt/twlv20-ai-skills/runtime
EnvironmentFile=/etc/twlv20-runtime/.env
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node dist/index.js
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal
NoNewPrivileges=true
ProtectSystem=strict
ProtectHome=true
ReadWritePaths=/opt/twlv20-ai-skills /var/log/twlv20-runtime

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

`/etc/twlv20-runtime/.env` holds the bootstrap secrets (Infisical machine identity client\_id + client\_secret); the rest is fetched from Infisical at runtime. mode 600.

### 8\. GitHub Actions secrets

In repo Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository secret:

```java
DROPLET_HOST          <droplet IP or hostname>
DROPLET_DEPLOY_USER   twlv20deploy
DROPLET_DEPLOY_KEY    (the private ed25519 key, full contents)
```

### 9\. Branch protection on `main`

Settings → Branches → Add rule, target `main`:

*   Require a pull request before merging (1 approver minimum).
*   Require status checks: `build`, `schema-check`.
*   Require branches to be up to date before merging.
*   Restrict who can push to matching branches: only deploy bots / repo admins.
*   Disallow force pushes.

### 10\. End-to-end no-op deploy

Open a no-op PR (e.g., a comment in [README.md](http://README.md)), wait for CI to go green, merge. Watch GitHub Actions; watch `journalctl -u twlv20-runtime.service -f` on the droplet. Confirm the service restarts and reports active.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Repo exists, scaffolded with the layout above, [README.md](http://README.md) committed.
- [ ] `.github/workflows/ci.yml` runs on PR; both `build` and `schema-check` jobs pass.
- [ ] `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` triggers on merge to `main`.
- [ ] `twlv20deploy` user on droplet, deploy key authorized, scoped sudo for `systemctl restart twlv20-runtime.service`.
- [ ] Branch protection enforced on `main`: PR + status checks + no force push.
- [ ] No-op deploy succeeded; `systemctl is-active twlv20-runtime.service` returns `active` post-deploy.
- [ ] Deploy private key never lived on the droplet; only the public key is on the droplet.

## Review gate

Jon opens a no-op PR, watches both CI jobs go green, merges, then watches the deploy workflow fire and the systemd service restart cleanly.

## Hazards

*   **Don't store the deploy private key on the droplet.** It lives in GitHub Secrets and on Keeper's laptop only.
*   **`appleboy/ssh-action`** **pinning** — pin to a specific tag or SHA, not `@v1` floating, to prevent supply-chain risk. Update intentionally.
*   **Don't put real secrets in** **`.env`** in the repo. The `.env` on the droplet is built from Infisical at deploy-time; the repo only carries `.env.example` with placeholders.
*   **Branch protection bypass** — keep "include administrators" enabled in branch protection so even an admin push goes through PR. Phase 1 is small enough that this is fine.
*   **`schema-check`** **job time** — Postgres + pgvector apt install adds ~2 min per CI run. If that becomes painful, build a custom Docker image and pre-install. Phase 1 don't optimize.

## Owner

Keeper.

## Depends on

GitHub repo provisioned (List 01); droplet hardening (Task 1); Postgres + RLS migration (Tasks 2 + 3 — the schema-check job depends on those).

## References

*   [appleboy/ssh-action (GitHub Marketplace)](https://github.com/marketplace/actions/ssh-deploy)
*   [GitHub Actions: SSH deploy with systemd](https://dev.to/lakhansamani/automate-server-deployment-with-systemd-github-actions-18od)
*   [GitHub Actions secrets docs](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-guides/encrypted-secrets)
*   [Branch protection rules](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-protected-branches/about-protected-branches)

tasks.name: Weekly encrypted Postgres snapshots → S3 / Backblaze B2
tasks.text_content:
Outcome

Every Sunday at 03:00 UTC, the droplet runs pg_dump over both twlv20 and infisical databases in custom format, encrypts each dump with GPG (symmetric, AES256), uploads to Backblaze B2 via rclone, retains the 8 most recent weekly snapshots, and emails on failure. A documented restore procedure has been exercised end-to-end on a scratch host.

Why this shape

pg_dump -Fc (custom format) — compressed by default, supports parallel restore via pg_restore -j, and lets you cherry-pick tables on restore. Plain SQL dumps work but can't parallelize and are 5–10× larger.
GPG symmetric (AES256), not asymmetric — one passphrase to manage, simpler operations. The passphrase lives in Infisical (Phase 1, post-Task-4) under backups.gpg.passphrase AND offline. We are guarding against B2 compromise or B2 lawful-access requests, not against an internal threat model where Infisical itself is compromised.
Backblaze B2 — cheaper than S3 for this access pattern, rclone supports it natively, and architecture §8 calls it out explicitly.
Weekly cadence matches contract §4 ("weekly snapshots"). For Phase 2 we add daily logical + WAL archiving for sub-hour RPO, but Phase 1 is weekly.

Pre-flight

Tasks 1, 2, 4 done (need Postgres running and Infisical to hold the GPG passphrase).
Twlv20 has a Backblaze B2 account; Keeper has API access and has created a private bucket called something like twlv20-postgres-backups-prod. Confirm with Jessie before creating new B2 accounts (paid).

Step-by-step

1. Install tooling

sudo apt -y install rclone gpg postgresql-client-16
rclone version
gpg --version
pg_dump --version

2. Configure rclone for B2

sudo -u root rclone config → n (new remote) → name b2-twlv20 → type b2 → enter Application Key ID + Application Key from B2.

The config lands at /root/.config/rclone/rclone.conf (root-readable only). Move it to /etc/rclone/rclone.conf and chmod 600.

sudo install -d /etc/rclone
sudo mv /root/.config/rclone/rclone.conf /etc/rclone/rclone.conf
sudo chmod 600 /etc/rclone/rclone.conf
sudo rclone --config /etc/rclone/rclone.conf lsd b2-twlv20:    # confirm bucket visible

3. Generate and store the GPG passphrase

PASSPHRASE=$(openssl rand -base64 48)
echo "$PASSPHRASE"

Store this immediately:

In Infisical, global project, key backups.gpg.passphrase (do this now, not later).
In Jon's offline 1Password, item "Twlv20 backup GPG passphrase".
Print on paper; store with Infisical encryption key (locked physically).

Without it, the encrypted dumps in B2 are a brick.

4. The backup script

Save as /usr/local/sbin/twlv20-backup.sh, mode 700, owner root:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# === Config ===
BUCKET="b2-twlv20:twlv20-postgres-backups-prod"
RETAIN=8
DATE=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H%M%SZ)
WORK_DIR=$(mktemp -d -t twlv20-backup.XXXXXX)
LOG=/var/log/twlv20-backup.log
PASSPHRASE_FILE=/run/twlv20-backup.passphrase   # tmpfs; wiped on reboot
ALERT_TO=ai@twlv20.com
DATABASES=(twlv20 infisical)

cleanup() {
  shred -u "$PASSPHRASE_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true
  rm -rf "$WORK_DIR"
}
trap cleanup EXIT

alert() {
  local subj="$1" body="$2"
  echo "$body" | mail -s "$subj" "$ALERT_TO" || true
}

# === Fetch passphrase from Infisical ===
# Requires `infisical` CLI configured with a machine identity scoped to global/backups
infisical secrets get backups.gpg.passphrase --plain > "$PASSPHRASE_FILE" || {
  alert "[CRIT] twlv20-backup: cannot fetch GPG passphrase" "Aborting backup."
  exit 2
}
chmod 600 "$PASSPHRASE_FILE"

# === Dump + encrypt + upload each DB ===
for db in "${DATABASES[@]}"; do
  out="$WORK_DIR/${db}-${DATE}.dump"
  enc="${out}.gpg"

  echo "[$(date -u --iso-8601=seconds)] dumping $db" | tee -a "$LOG"
  pg_dump -h 127.0.0.1 -U postgres -d "$db" -Fc -f "$out"

  echo "[$(date -u --iso-8601=seconds)] encrypting $db" | tee -a "$LOG"
  gpg --batch --yes --symmetric --cipher-algo AES256 \
      --passphrase-file "$PASSPHRASE_FILE" \
      --output "$enc" \
      "$out"

  echo "[$(date -u --iso-8601=seconds)] uploading $db" | tee -a "$LOG"
  rclone --config /etc/rclone/rclone.conf copyto "$enc" \
         "$BUCKET/${db}/$(basename "$enc")" \
         --b2-hard-delete=false

  shred -u "$out"
done

# === Retention: keep RETAIN newest per DB ===
for db in "${DATABASES[@]}"; do
  rclone --config /etc/rclone/rclone.conf lsf "$BUCKET/$db/" \
    | sort \
    | head -n "-$RETAIN" \
    | while read -r old; do
        echo "[$(date -u --iso-8601=seconds)] pruning $old" | tee -a "$LOG"
        rclone --config /etc/rclone/rclone.conf delete "$BUCKET/$db/$old"
      done
done

echo "[$(date -u --iso-8601=seconds)] backup OK" | tee -a "$LOG"

5. Postgres credentials for the script

Postgres-as-postgres runs the dump locally (peer auth via Unix socket — no password needed if the script runs as root via systemd). Confirm:

sudo -u postgres pg_dump twlv20 -Fc -f /tmp/test.dump && rm /tmp/test.dump

If peer auth doesn't apply (e.g., script runs as a non-postgres user), use ~/.pgpass mode 600 with the postgres user's password instead.

6. systemd timer (preferred over crontab)

/etc/systemd/system/twlv20-backup.service:

[Unit]
Description=Twlv20 weekly Postgres backup to B2
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target postgresql.service

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/usr/local/sbin/twlv20-backup.sh
Nice=10
IOSchedulingClass=best-effort
IOSchedulingPriority=7
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal

/etc/systemd/system/twlv20-backup.timer:

[Unit]
Description=Run twlv20 backup weekly

[Timer]
OnCalendar=Sun *-*-* 03:00:00 UTC
Persistent=true
RandomizedDelaySec=300

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target

Activate:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now twlv20-backup.timer
sudo systemctl list-timers twlv20-backup.timer

7. First-run smoke test

Trigger the timer's service manually:

sudo systemctl start twlv20-backup.service
sudo journalctl -u twlv20-backup.service --no-pager
sudo rclone --config /etc/rclone/rclone.conf ls b2-twlv20:twlv20-postgres-backups-prod/

You should see two encrypted files (one per DB) in the bucket.

8. Restore drill (the acceptance event)

Provision a tiny scratch droplet (or local VM). Install Postgres 16. Then:

# Pull the latest backup
rclone copy b2-twlv20:twlv20-postgres-backups-prod/twlv20/ /tmp/restore/ --include "*.gpg" --max-age 8d

# Decrypt
gpg --batch --yes --decrypt --passphrase-file /run/passphrase \
    --output /tmp/restore/twlv20.dump \
    /tmp/restore/twlv20-*.dump.gpg

# Restore into a scratch DB
createdb twlv20_restore_test
pg_restore -d twlv20_restore_test -j 4 -v /tmp/restore/twlv20.dump

# Spot-check
psql -d twlv20_restore_test -c "SELECT count(*) FROM tenants;"
psql -d twlv20_restore_test -c "SELECT count(*) FROM runs;"

If tenants returns 4 and runs returns whatever was in production, the restore is valid. Tear down the scratch host. Document the row counts in the task comment.

9. Failure-alert verification

Force a failure to confirm the alert path works:

# Temporarily break it: rename the script to twlv20-backup.sh.bak, replace with `exit 1`
sudo systemctl start twlv20-backup.service
# Confirm an email lands in ai@twlv20.com from the local mail daemon
# Restore the original script

If mail isn't configured, install bsd-mailx + a relay (e.g., msmtp configured against ai@twlv20.com's SMTP).

Jon does the restore drill himself on a scratch host and runs the row-count spot-check.

rclone config holds B2 application keys — chmod 600 it.
shred -u the plain .dump file before the encrypted upload, and shred the passphrase tmpfs file on exit. The script's cleanup trap handles this.

Owner

Keeper.

Depends on

Postgres install (Task 2), Infisical install (Task 4 — for passphrase storage).

References

pg_dump official docs
rclone B2 setup (community guide)
pg_dump best practices (Microsoft / Azure)
GPG + rclone backup pattern

sudo apt -y install rclone gpg postgresql-client-16

gpg --version

sudo -u root rclone config → n (new remote) → name b2-twlv20 → type b2 → enter Application Key ID + Application Key from B2.

The config lands at /root/.config/rclone/rclone.conf (root-readable only). Move it to /etc/rclone/rclone.conf and chmod 600.

sudo install -d /etc/rclone
sudo mv /root/.config/rclone/rclone.conf /etc/rclone/rclone.conf
sudo chmod 600 /etc/rclone/rclone.conf
sudo rclone --config /etc/rclone/rclone.conf lsd b2-twlv20:    # confirm bucket visible

3. Generate and store the GPG passphrase

PASSPHRASE=$(openssl rand -base64 48)
echo "$PASSPHRASE"

Store this immediately:

In Infisical, global project, key backups.gpg.passphrase (do this now, not later).
In Jon's offline 1Password, item "Twlv20 backup GPG passphrase".
Print on paper; store with Infisical encryption key (locked physically).

Without it, the encrypted dumps in B2 are a brick.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# === Config ===
BUCKET="b2-twlv20:twlv20-postgres-backups-prod"
RETAIN=8
DATE=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H%M%SZ)
WORK_DIR=$(mktemp -d -t twlv20-backup.XXXXXX)
LOG=/var/log/twlv20-backup.log
PASSPHRASE_FILE=/run/twlv20-backup.passphrase   # tmpfs; wiped on reboot
ALERT_TO=ai@twlv20.com
DATABASES=(twlv20 infisical)

cleanup() {
  shred -u "$PASSPHRASE_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true
  rm -rf "$WORK_DIR"
}
trap cleanup EXIT

alert() {
  local subj="$1" body="$2"
  echo "$body" | mail -s "$subj" "$ALERT_TO" || true
}

# === Fetch passphrase from Infisical ===
# Requires `infisical` CLI configured with a machine identity scoped to global/backups
infisical secrets get backups.gpg.passphrase --plain > "$PASSPHRASE_FILE" || {
  alert "[CRIT] twlv20-backup: cannot fetch GPG passphrase" "Aborting backup."
  exit 2
}
chmod 600 "$PASSPHRASE_FILE"

# === Dump + encrypt + upload each DB ===
for db in "${DATABASES[@]}"; do
  out="$WORK_DIR/${db}-${DATE}.dump"
  enc="${out}.gpg"

  echo "[$(date -u --iso-8601=seconds)] dumping $db" | tee -a "$LOG"
  pg_dump -h 127.0.0.1 -U postgres -d "$db" -Fc -f "$out"

  echo "[$(date -u --iso-8601=seconds)] encrypting $db" | tee -a "$LOG"
  gpg --batch --yes --symmetric --cipher-algo AES256 \
      --passphrase-file "$PASSPHRASE_FILE" \
      --output "$enc" \
      "$out"

  echo "[$(date -u --iso-8601=seconds)] uploading $db" | tee -a "$LOG"
  rclone --config /etc/rclone/rclone.conf copyto "$enc" \
         "$BUCKET/${db}/$(basename "$enc")" \
         --b2-hard-delete=false

  shred -u "$out"
done

for db in "${DATABASES[@]}"; do
  rclone --config /etc/rclone/rclone.conf lsf "$BUCKET/$db/" \
    | sort \
    | head -n "-$RETAIN" \
    | while read -r old; do
        echo "[$(date -u --iso-8601=seconds)] pruning $old" | tee -a "$LOG"
        rclone --config /etc/rclone/rclone.conf delete "$BUCKET/$db/$old"
      done
done

echo "[$(date -u --iso-8601=seconds)] backup OK" | tee -a "$LOG"

5. Postgres credentials for the script

Postgres-as-postgres runs the dump locally (peer auth via Unix socket — no password needed if the script runs as root via systemd). Confirm:

sudo -u postgres pg_dump twlv20 -Fc -f /tmp/test.dump && rm /tmp/test.dump

If peer auth doesn't apply (e.g., script runs as a non-postgres user), use ~/.pgpass mode 600 with the postgres user's password instead.

/etc/systemd/system/twlv20-backup.service:

[Unit]
Description=Twlv20 weekly Postgres backup to B2
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target postgresql.service

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/usr/local/sbin/twlv20-backup.sh
Nice=10
IOSchedulingClass=best-effort
IOSchedulingPriority=7
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal

/etc/systemd/system/twlv20-backup.timer:

[Unit]
Description=Run twlv20 backup weekly

[Timer]
OnCalendar=Sun *-*-* 03:00:00 UTC
Persistent=true
RandomizedDelaySec=300

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target

Activate:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now twlv20-backup.timer
sudo systemctl list-timers twlv20-backup.timer

7. First-run smoke test

Trigger the timer's service manually:

sudo systemctl start twlv20-backup.service
sudo journalctl -u twlv20-backup.service --no-pager
sudo rclone --config /etc/rclone/rclone.conf ls b2-twlv20:twlv20-postgres-backups-prod/

tasks.name: Infisical — self-hosted install + per-tenant scoping
tasks.text_content:
Outcome

Self-hosted Infisical runs on the droplet behind Caddy at infisical.<domain>, with one project per tenant + one global project, machine identities scoped per tenant, and the encryption key backed up offline. Phase 1 secrets that currently live in a password manager (DO password, app DB pw, API keys from List 01) get migrated in. The agent runtime fetches secrets at request time using a tenant-scoped machine identity.

Architectural notes (so Keeper isn't picking shape blindly)

Infisical needs its own Postgres database and Redis alongside the app. Reusing Twlv20's existing Postgres (Task 2) is acceptable for Phase 1 to avoid a second DB engine — create a separate infisical database in the same instance with its own owner role. Redis is new; install via apt.
Deploy via docker-compose using the official docker-compose.prod.yml from the Infisical repo as the starting point. Don't run via docker run directly — compose manages the dependency graph.
The encryption key in .env is the master key for at-rest secret encryption. Losing it = losing every secret. It does NOT live in Infisical itself (chicken-and-egg). It lives in /etc/infisical/.env (root-only readable on the droplet) AND a copy in your offline 1Password vault, AND a printed paper copy somewhere safe. Re-emphasis: back it up before storing the first real secret.
Service tokens are deprecated as of 2024 in favor of Machine Identities with auth methods (Universal Auth, Token Auth, etc.). Use Machine Identities throughout; do not provision new Service Tokens.

Pre-flight

Tasks 1, 2 done.
Caddy block for infisical.<domain> already in /etc/caddy/Caddyfile with reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:8080 (set up in Task 1, step 6).
Docker + docker-compose installed (apt install docker.io docker-compose-v2).

Step-by-step

1. Provision Infisical's Postgres database

In the existing Postgres instance (Task 2):

sudo -u postgres psql <<'SQL'
CREATE ROLE infisical_app LOGIN PASSWORD :'pw';
ALTER ROLE infisical_app NOSUPERUSER NOCREATEDB NOCREATEROLE NOBYPASSRLS;
CREATE DATABASE infisical OWNER infisical_app;
SQL

Generate pw with openssl rand -base64 32. Keep separate from the twlv20_app password.

2. Install Redis (apt, localhost-only)

sudo apt -y install redis-server
sudo sed -i 's/^bind .*/bind 127.0.0.1 ::1/' /etc/redis/redis.conf
sudo sed -i 's/^# requirepass .*/requirepass <generated-redis-pw>/' /etc/redis/redis.conf
sudo systemctl restart redis-server
sudo systemctl enable redis-server
ss -tlnp | grep 6379    # confirm 127.0.0.1 only

3. Pull the Infisical repo and prepare the env

sudo mkdir -p /opt/infisical && cd /opt/infisical
sudo git clone https://github.com/Infisical/infisical.git source
sudo cp source/docker-compose.prod.yml docker-compose.yml

Create /opt/infisical/.env (root-only):

sudo touch /etc/infisical/.env || sudo install -d /etc/infisical
sudo tee /etc/infisical/.env <<EOF >/dev/null
# === SECURITY KEYS — back up offline before first secret is stored ===
ENCRYPTION_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 16)
AUTH_SECRET=$(openssl rand -base64 32)

# === DB ===
DB_CONNECTION_URI=postgres://infisical_app:<infisical_app_pw>@host.docker.internal:5432/infisical?sslmode=disable

# === Redis ===
REDIS_URL=redis://:<redis-pw>@host.docker.internal:6379

# === App ===
NODE_ENV=production
SITE_URL=https://infisical.<your-domain>

# === SMTP (optional but enables invites/resets) ===
SMTP_HOST=smtp.gmail.com
SMTP_PORT=587
SMTP_USERNAME=ai@twlv20.com
SMTP_PASSWORD=<app-password>
SMTP_FROM_ADDRESS=ai@twlv20.com
SMTP_FROM_NAME=Twlv20 Infisical
EOF
sudo chmod 600 /etc/infisical/.env

Immediately copy ENCRYPTION_KEY and AUTH_SECRET to:

1Password (or equivalent) under "Twlv20 Infisical encryption key — DO NOT LOSE".
Print on paper, lock physically.

If Keeper loses these and the DB is corrupted, every secret is unrecoverable.

4. Wire docker-compose to use the host's Postgres + Redis

Edit /opt/infisical/docker-compose.yml to:

Point infisical-backend env to /etc/infisical/.env.
Remove the bundled db and redis services (we're using host versions).
Add extra_hosts: ["host.docker.internal:host-gateway"] so the container can resolve the host's Postgres/Redis.
Bind only 127.0.0.1:8080:8080 for the backend port (Caddy proxies it).

The exact YAML diff depends on the current upstream compose file — pull and inspect at deploy time. Reference: https://github.com/Infisical/infisical/blob/main/docker-compose.prod.yml.

5. Bring it up

cd /opt/infisical
sudo docker compose --env-file /etc/infisical/.env up -d
sudo docker compose ps
sudo docker compose logs -f --tail 100   # watch for migration completion + 'listening on 8080'

The first user to register becomes the org owner. Register ai@twlv20.com via the UI.

[table-embed:1:1 Project| 1:2 Purpose| 2:1 global | 2:2 Shared agency-wide secrets (Smart Lead API, ScaledMail when added)| 3:1 pure-peptide | 3:2 Pure Peptide Solutions secrets| 4:1 ajiri | 4:2 Ajiri Sciences secrets| 5:1 sru | 5:2 SRU secrets| 6:1 twlv20-internal | 6:2 Twlv20 Internal secrets|]

UI path: Org Settings → Access Control → Identities → Add Identity. Use Universal Auth for each:

Auth method:           Universal Auth
Client ID:             (auto-generated, save)
Client Secret:         (auto-generated, save once — store in /etc/infisical/.env-machine-ids)
Token TTL:             1h
Access Token Max TTL:  24h

openai.api.key — Whisper / OpenAI key
smartlead.api.key — Smart Lead key

vercel.api.token
do.droplet.ip and do.api.token

redis.password

Reads a global secret with the global identity → succeeds.
Reads a pure-peptide secret with the pure-peptide identity → succeeds.
Tries to read an ajiri secret with the pure-peptide identity → fails (403).

Infisical service token deprecation notice
docker-compose.prod.yml in upstream repo

tasks.name: Tenant schema + RLS migration (per Plinko spec)
tasks.text_content:
Outcome

Plinko's infra/schema.sql migration is applied to the twlv20 database. Every tenant-scoped table carries tenant_id, has RLS enabled, has policies that key reads/writes on current_setting('app.tenant_id'), and FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is set so the table owner is also subject to policies. Four tenant rows exist. An RLS isolation test passes for all four tenants.

How RLS will be used (so Keeper isn't guessing)

The agent runtime (twlv20_app role, NOBYPASSRLS) opens a Postgres connection per request and immediately runs SELECT set_config('app.tenant_id', '<tenant-uuid>', false); before any query. Every policy on every tenant-scoped table compares tenant_id::text to that GUC. If the runtime forgets to set the GUC, queries return zero rows (the GUC defaults to empty, no row matches) — fail-closed, not fail-open. Plinko owns the runtime side of this contract; Keeper's job is to make sure the SQL side is bulletproof.

Pre-flight

Task 2 (Postgres + pgvector) shipped.
Plinko has dropped infra/schema.sql into the twlv20-ai-skills repo (or attached to this ClickUp task as a file). If absent at the time Keeper picks this up, comment on the task and block until Plinko delivers.

Tables in scope (per architecture §6)

tenants, users, credentials, skills, kb_docs, agents, runs (the Run-Log), approvals, artifacts. tenants is the only table NOT keyed by tenant_id (the table itself enumerates them) and gets a different policy.

Step-by-step

1. Apply the migration

Pull the latest infra/schema.sql from the repo, then:

psql -h 127.0.0.1 -U twlv20_app -d twlv20 -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 -f infra/schema.sql

If the migration fails partway through, drop the DB and recreate (still empty at this point), then re-run. No rollback ladder is needed yet — we're pre-production.

2. Verify the schema landed

\dt          -- expect: tenants, users, credentials, skills, kb_docs, agents, runs, approvals, artifacts
\d runs      -- inspect Run-Log columns; must match architecture §6 schema:
             -- run_id, tenant_id, workflow, agent, timestamp, trigger_source,
             -- input_hash, confidence_score, HIL_status, outcome, token_cost,
             -- api_cost, duration_ms, artifact_refs, error_payload

If any column or table is missing, file the diff back to Plinko via task comment — don't paper over schema gaps.

3. Verify RLS is on for every tenant-scoped table

SELECT c.relname,
       c.relrowsecurity AS rls_enabled,
       c.relforcerowsecurity AS force_rls
FROM pg_class c
JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = c.relnamespace
WHERE n.nspname = 'public'
  AND c.relkind = 'r'
ORDER BY c.relname;

Expected: every table except tenants should have both rls_enabled = true AND force_rls = true. FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is critical — without it, the table owner (often twlv20_app if it owns the table) bypasses the policies.

If any row shows force_rls = false, run:

ALTER TABLE <table_name> FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

4. Verify the policy shape

For each tenant-scoped table, the policy should look like one of these patterns. Confirm with \d+ <table> per table.

-- Read policy
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation_select ON runs
    FOR SELECT TO twlv20_app
    USING (tenant_id::text = current_setting('app.tenant_id', true));

-- Write policy (insert/update/delete must also enforce)
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation_modify ON runs
    FOR ALL TO twlv20_app
    USING (tenant_id::text = current_setting('app.tenant_id', true))
    WITH CHECK (tenant_id::text = current_setting('app.tenant_id', true));

The , true arg to current_setting makes it return empty string instead of erroring when the GUC isn't set — that's intentional, the empty-string path returns zero rows (fail-closed).

For tenants itself, a different policy lets the app role read its own tenant row but no others:

CREATE POLICY self_only ON tenants
    FOR SELECT TO twlv20_app
    USING (id::text = current_setting('app.tenant_id', true));

5. Insert the four tenant rows

INSERT INTO tenants (id, slug, display_name, created_at) VALUES
  (gen_random_uuid(), 'pure-peptide',   'Pure Peptide Solutions', now()),
  (gen_random_uuid(), 'ajiri',          'Ajiri Sciences',         now()),
  (gen_random_uuid(), 'sru',            'Sales Recruiting University', now()),
  (gen_random_uuid(), 'twlv20-internal','Twlv20 Internal',        now())
RETURNING id, slug;

Capture the returned UUIDs — Task 4 (Infisical) and List 03 → "Stub four tenants in DB" reuse them.

6. Isolation test (the acceptance gate)

Save as infra/tests/rls_isolation.sql:

-- Run as twlv20_app
\set ON_ERROR_STOP on

-- Pick a tenant
SELECT id AS pure_peptide_id FROM tenants WHERE slug = 'pure-peptide' \gset
SELECT id AS ajiri_id        FROM tenants WHERE slug = 'ajiri' \gset

-- Set context to Pure Peptide
SELECT set_config('app.tenant_id', :'pure_peptide_id', false);

-- Insert a run row attributed to Pure Peptide
INSERT INTO runs (run_id, tenant_id, workflow, agent, trigger_source, hil_status)
VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), :'pure_peptide_id', 'isolation-test', 'rls-test', 'manual', 'auto');

-- Confirm we can read it
SELECT count(*) AS pp_visible FROM runs;   -- expect 1

-- Switch to Ajiri
SELECT set_config('app.tenant_id', :'ajiri_id', false);
SELECT count(*) AS ajiri_visible FROM runs;   -- expect 0  ← THE PROOF

-- Try to insert a row for Pure Peptide while in Ajiri context (must fail)
DO $$
BEGIN
  BEGIN
    INSERT INTO runs (run_id, tenant_id, workflow, agent, trigger_source, hil_status)
    VALUES (gen_random_uuid(),
            (SELECT id FROM tenants WHERE slug='pure-peptide'),
            'cross-tenant-injection', 'rls-test', 'manual', 'auto');
    RAISE EXCEPTION 'RLS FAILURE: cross-tenant insert succeeded';
  EXCEPTION WHEN insufficient_privilege OR check_violation THEN
    RAISE NOTICE 'RLS OK: cross-tenant insert blocked';
  END;
END $$;

-- Cleanup
SELECT set_config('app.tenant_id', :'pure_peptide_id', false);
DELETE FROM runs WHERE workflow = 'isolation-test';

Run as twlv20_app:

psql -h 127.0.0.1 -U twlv20_app -d twlv20 -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 -f infra/tests/rls_isolation.sql

Acceptance: pp_visible = 1, ajiri_visible = 0, and the NOTICE "RLS OK" appears.

7. Re-run the matrix

For thoroughness, run the same test pairwise across all 4 tenants (12 ordered pairs). A small Python or bash wrapper around psql is fine. Record results in the task comment.

Acceptance criteria

\dt shows all tables from architecture §6.
Every tenant-scoped table has relrowsecurity = true and relforcerowsecurity = true.
Policies on every tenant-scoped table reference current_setting('app.tenant_id', true).
4 tenant UUIDs captured (post the slug → UUID mapping in the task comment for Plinko + Infisical task).
Isolation test passes for every pairwise combination.
Run-Log column list matches architecture §6 exactly — file diffs back to Plinko if they don't.

Review gate

Jon reviews the diff between the merged migration and the spec doc, runs the full pairwise isolation matrix, and signs off. This is a security-critical gate — don't ship if any pair fails.

Hazards

twlv20_app cannot have BYPASSRLS. Verified in Task 2; verify again here with \du twlv20_app.
FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is required because if twlv20_app owns the tables, it bypasses RLS by default. The migration should set this on every table; verify in step 3.
GUC reset between connections. A pooled connection may carry over app.tenant_id from a prior request. Plinko's runtime is responsible for resetting on every checkout; Keeper's job is just to make sure the policies are correct so a misconfigured runtime fails closed (zero rows), not open.
current_setting('app.tenant_id', true) — the , true is essential. Without it, an unset GUC throws unrecognized configuration parameter and the whole query fails noisily. With it, the empty-string default → zero rows.
Don't \dx the tenants table without RLS — it must remain visible cross-tenant for the runtime's tenant-lookup step (which happens before the GUC is set). The self_only policy in step 4 handles this carefully; verify it specifically.

Owner

Keeper.

Depends on

Postgres + pgvector install (Task 2); Plinko's infra/schema.sql.

Blocks

Stub four tenants (List 03); every channel integration that writes to the DB; Run-Log emission verification.

References

PostgreSQL Row Security Policies (16 docs)
Common RLS footguns (Bytebase)
Multi-tenant RLS pattern (AWS blog)
RLS for tenants in Postgres (Crunchy Data)

### 7\. Re-run the matrix

- [ ] Every tenant-scoped table has `relrowsecurity = true` and `relforcerowsecurity = true`.

tasks.name: Postgres 16 + pgvector install
tasks.text_content:
Outcome

PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector running on the hardened droplet, listening on the Unix socket and 127.0.0.1 only, with a non-superuser application role and tuning matched to 16 GB RAM. This is the data spine the Run-Log, tenant schema, and Infisical all sit on.

Pre-flight

Task 1 (Droplet hardening) shipped.
DNS not relevant — Postgres never speaks publicly.

Step-by-step

1. Add the PGDG official apt repo (deb822 format)

Ubuntu 24.04 ships an older Postgres in its default archive. Use the PostgreSQL Global Development Group repo for current 16.x patches.

sudo apt -y install curl ca-certificates
sudo install -d /usr/share/postgresql-common/pgdg
sudo curl -o /usr/share/postgresql-common/pgdg/apt.postgresql.org.asc \
  --fail https://www.postgresql.org/media/keys/ACCC4CF8.asc

sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pgdg.sources <<'EOF'
Types: deb
URIs: https://apt.postgresql.org/pub/repos/apt
Suites: noble-pgdg
Architectures: amd64
Components: main
Signed-By: /usr/share/postgresql-common/pgdg/apt.postgresql.org.asc
EOF

sudo apt update

2. Install Postgres 16 + pgvector

sudo apt -y install postgresql-16 postgresql-client-16 postgresql-16-pgvector
psql -V          # confirm 16.x
ls -l /usr/lib/postgresql/16/lib/vector.so   # confirm pgvector library present

3. Create the application role and the main database

Postgres-as-postgres:

sudo -u postgres psql <<'SQL'
CREATE ROLE twlv20_app LOGIN PASSWORD :'app_pw';
ALTER ROLE twlv20_app NOSUPERUSER NOCREATEDB NOCREATEROLE NOBYPASSRLS;
CREATE DATABASE twlv20 OWNER twlv20_app;
\c twlv20
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pgcrypto;
SQL

You'll be prompted for app_pw — generate with openssl rand -base64 32 and store it in Infisical (post-Task-4) under db.postgres.app_user. For the pre-Infisical interim, save it in your password manager.

NOBYPASSRLS is critical — the application role must be subject to row-level security policies (Task 3 builds those).

4. Lock listener and pg_hba.conf to local-only

Postgres 16 listens on localhost by default but pg_hba.conf ships with broader rules that should be tightened.

/etc/postgresql/16/main/postgresql.conf:

listen_addresses = 'localhost'
unix_socket_directories = '/var/run/postgresql'
ssl = on

/etc/postgresql/16/main/pg_hba.conf — replace the contents with:

# TYPE    DATABASE  USER         ADDRESS         METHOD
local     all       postgres                     peer
local     all       all                          scram-sha-256
host      all       all          127.0.0.1/32    scram-sha-256
host      all       all          ::1/128         scram-sha-256
# explicitly no remote rules — agent runtime is on the same droplet

Reload:

sudo systemctl reload postgresql@16-main

Verify nothing external is reachable:

ss -tlnp | grep 5432   # bind only on 127.0.0.1:5432

effective_cache_size = 8GB
maintenance_work_mem = 512MB
work_mem = 32MB
wal_buffers = 16MB

# Checkpoint / WAL
checkpoint_completion_target = 0.9
max_wal_size = 4GB
min_wal_size = 512MB

# Planner
random_page_cost = 1.1            # NVMe storage
effective_io_concurrency = 200    # NVMe

# Parallel workers (8 vCPU droplet)
max_worker_processes = 8
max_parallel_workers = 8
max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 2
max_parallel_maintenance_workers = 4

# Logging
log_min_duration_statement = 1000  # log queries > 1s
log_checkpoints = on
log_connections = on
log_lock_waits = on
log_temp_files = 0

# Connection limit
max_connections = 100

CREATE TABLE _vector_smoke (id serial primary key, embedding vector(3));
INSERT INTO _vector_smoke (embedding) VALUES ('[1,2,3]'), ('[4,5,6]');
SELECT id, embedding <-> '[1,2,3]' AS l2 FROM _vector_smoke ORDER BY l2;
DROP TABLE _vector_smoke;

SHOW shared_buffers; → 4GB. SHOW effective_cache_size; → 8GB. SHOW work_mem; → 32MB.
pgvector smoke test (step 6) returns the two expected rows.
App password stored in password manager; will move to Infisical at Task 4.

Review gate

Jon connects as twlv20_app from the droplet, runs the pgvector smoke test, runs \du twlv20_app and verifies the NOBYPASSRLS flag, and tries to connect from an external IP (which must fail).

Don't install pgvector from source — the apt package keeps step pace with Postgres 16 updates and cleanly handles pg_upgrade.
Don't give twlv20_app BYPASSRLS or superuser. Task 3 won't work — superuser bypasses RLS by default.
Don't allow remote Postgres access "for convenience" — the agent runtime lives on the same droplet, and any future remote need goes through OpenClaw, never directly.

Keeper.

tasks.name: Droplet hardening — Caddy/TLS, SSH-key-only, fail2ban
tasks.text_content:
Outcome

A DigitalOcean droplet (Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, 8 vCPU / 16 GB / NVMe) running Caddy as the public-facing reverse proxy with automatic TLS, with SSH locked to keys-only, root login disabled, fail2ban watching SSH + Caddy logs, and UFW configured. This is the substrate every other List 02 task installs onto.

Pre-flight

DigitalOcean account exists, Keeper is admin (List 01 → "DigitalOcean — droplet account").
Droplet provisioned: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, 8 vCPU / 16 GB / NVMe, region chosen for latency.
DNS for the chosen hostname pointed at the droplet IP. Suggested subdomains: paperclip.<domain>, claw.<domain>, infisical.<domain> — all pointing at the same droplet, Caddy routes by Host header.
Local SSH keypair generated for keeper and jon (Ed25519: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C keeper@twlv20).

Step-by-step

1. First-login hygiene

ssh root@<droplet-ip>
apt update && apt -y full-upgrade && apt -y autoremove
apt -y install ufw fail2ban curl ca-certificates debian-keyring debian-archive-keyring apt-transport-https unattended-upgrades
dpkg-reconfigure -plow unattended-upgrades   # enable auto security updates
hostnamectl set-hostname twlv20-prod
timedatectl set-timezone UTC

2. Create non-root sudo users

adduser keeper      # set strong password, you'll only use it for sudo
adduser jon
usermod -aG sudo keeper
usermod -aG sudo jon
mkdir -p /home/keeper/.ssh /home/jon/.ssh
# paste each user's public key into their authorized_keys
chmod 700 /home/keeper/.ssh /home/jon/.ssh
chmod 600 /home/keeper/.ssh/authorized_keys /home/jon/.ssh/authorized_keys
chown -R keeper:keeper /home/keeper/.ssh
chown -R jon:jon /home/jon/.ssh

Open a second terminal and verify ssh keeper@<droplet-ip> and ssh jon@<droplet-ip> work with keys before you continue. If either fails, fix before locking down sshd or you will lock yourself out.

3. Harden sshd (drop-in file pattern)

Use /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/99-hardening.conf rather than editing the main config — Ubuntu 24.04's main config includes the drop-in directory and this style is forward-compatible.

cat <<'EOF' > /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/99-hardening.conf
PermitRootLogin no
PasswordAuthentication no
ChallengeResponseAuthentication no
KbdInteractiveAuthentication no
UsePAM yes
PubkeyAuthentication yes
PermitEmptyPasswords no
MaxAuthTries 3
LoginGraceTime 20
ClientAliveInterval 300
ClientAliveCountMax 2
AllowUsers keeper jon
Protocol 2
EOF
sshd -t   # syntax check; refuse to restart if this errors
systemctl restart ssh

Confirm from your second terminal that you can still log in. If you cannot, the original session is your escape hatch.

4. UFW

ufw limit on SSH adds rate-limiting for brute-force at the firewall layer (in addition to fail2ban):

ufw default deny incoming
ufw default allow outgoing
ufw limit 22/tcp comment 'SSH rate-limited'
ufw allow 80/tcp comment 'HTTP (Caddy ACME + redirect)'
ufw allow 443/tcp comment 'HTTPS (Caddy)'
ufw --force enable
ufw status verbose

Caddy needs port 80 open for ACME HTTP-01 challenges and to redirect to HTTPS. No other ports.

5. Caddy install via official apt repo

curl -1sLf 'https://dl.cloudsmith.io/public/caddy/stable/gpg.key' | gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/caddy-stable-archive-keyring.gpg
curl -1sLf 'https://dl.cloudsmith.io/public/caddy/stable/debian.deb.txt' | tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/caddy-stable.list
apt update
apt -y install caddy

The package installs caddy.service, creates the caddy user/group, and starts the service. Confirm:

systemctl status caddy   # active (running)
caddy version            # v2.x.x

6. Caddyfile baseline

Edit /etc/caddy/Caddyfile. The baseline below routes three subdomains, enables JSON access logs (we'll feed those to fail2ban in step 8), and sets sensible timeouts:

{
    email ai@twlv20.com
    log {
        output stderr
        format json
    }
    servers {
        timeouts {
            read_body 30s
            read_header 10s
            write 30s
            idle 5m
        }
    }
}

paperclip.<your-domain> {
    encode zstd gzip
    log {
        output file /var/log/caddy/paperclip-access.log {
            roll_size 50mb
            roll_keep 7
        }
        format json
    }
    reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:3000   # Paperclip default port; List 03 will install
}

claw.<your-domain> {
    encode zstd gzip
    log {
        output file /var/log/caddy/claw-access.log {
            roll_size 50mb
            roll_keep 7
        }
        format json
    }
    reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:4000   # OpenClaw; List 03 installs
}

infisical.<your-domain> {
    encode zstd gzip
    log {
        output file /var/log/caddy/infisical-access.log {
            roll_size 50mb
            roll_keep 7
        }
        format json
    }
    reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:8080   # Infisical; List 02 task 4 installs
}

Replace <your-domain> with the chosen domain. Reload:

mkdir -p /var/log/caddy && chown caddy:caddy /var/log/caddy
caddy validate --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
systemctl reload caddy
journalctl -u caddy -n 50 --no-pager   # confirm no errors; confirm cert obtained

Each subdomain will return a 502 until its upstream exists — that's expected. The TLS cert acquisition is the proof point at this stage.

7. fail2ban — SSH jail

cat <<'EOF' > /etc/fail2ban/jail.local
[DEFAULT]
bantime = 1h
findtime = 10m
maxretry = 5
backend = systemd
ignoreip = 127.0.0.1/8 ::1

[sshd]
enabled = true
port = ssh
filter = sshd
maxretry = 3
findtime = 5m
bantime = 24h
EOF
systemctl restart fail2ban
fail2ban-client status sshd

8. fail2ban — Caddy access-log jail

Caddy logs JSON; the simplest approach is a regex filter that matches the JSON status field for repeated 401/403/404 from the same IP. Drop in:

cat <<'EOF' > /etc/fail2ban/filter.d/caddy-status.conf
[Definition]
failregex = ^.*"remote_ip":"<HOST>".*"status":(401|403|404).*$
ignoreregex =
EOF

cat <<'EOF' >> /etc/fail2ban/jail.local

[caddy-status]
enabled = true
filter = caddy-status
logpath = /var/log/caddy/*-access.log
maxretry = 20
findtime = 5m
bantime = 1h
port = http,https
EOF
systemctl restart fail2ban
fail2ban-client status caddy-status

Tune maxretry after a week of real traffic — 20 in 5 minutes is a starting point that won't false-positive on legitimate users hitting 404s.

A more sophisticated approach is the Javex/caddy-fail2ban plugin (built via xcaddy) where Caddy itself enforces the ban list. Defer that to Phase 2 unless we hit a real abuse pattern.

9. Final lockdown

passwd -l root           # disable root password login entirely
systemctl enable --now ufw fail2ban caddy unattended-upgrades

Acceptance criteria

ssh root@<ip> denied; ssh keeper@<ip> and ssh jon@<ip> succeed with key only.
sudo cat /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/99-hardening.conf shows PermitRootLogin no and PasswordAuthentication no.
sudo ufw status verbose shows: 22/tcp LIMIT, 80/tcp ALLOW, 443/tcp ALLOW, default deny incoming.
systemctl status caddy → active (running); curl -I https://<one-subdomain> returns a Let's Encrypt-signed TLS handshake (502 on the body is fine — upstream not yet installed).
fail2ban-client status sshd → active jail.

Local SSH keypair generated for keeper and jon (Ed25519: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C keeper@twlv20).

1. First-login hygiene

apt update && apt -y full-upgrade && apt -y autoremove
apt -y install ufw fail2ban curl ca-certificates debian-keyring debian-archive-keyring apt-transport-https unattended-upgrades
dpkg-reconfigure -plow unattended-upgrades   # enable auto security updates
hostnamectl set-hostname twlv20-prod
timedatectl set-timezone UTC

2. Create non-root sudo users

adduser keeper      # set strong password, you'll only use it for sudo
adduser jon
usermod -aG sudo keeper
usermod -aG sudo jon
mkdir -p /home/keeper/.ssh /home/jon/.ssh

chmod 700 /home/keeper/.ssh /home/jon/.ssh
chmod 600 /home/keeper/.ssh/authorized_keys /home/jon/.ssh/authorized_keys
chown -R keeper:keeper /home/keeper/.ssh
chown -R jon:jon /home/jon/.ssh

Open a second terminal and verify ssh keeper@<droplet-ip> and ssh jon@<droplet-ip> work with keys before you continue. If either fails, fix before locking down sshd or you will lock yourself out.

3. Harden sshd (drop-in file pattern)

Use /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/99-hardening.conf rather than editing the main config — Ubuntu 24.04's main config includes the drop-in directory and this style is forward-compatible.

cat <<'EOF' > /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/99-hardening.conf
PermitRootLogin no
PasswordAuthentication no
ChallengeResponseAuthentication no
KbdInteractiveAuthentication no
UsePAM yes
PubkeyAuthentication yes
PermitEmptyPasswords no
MaxAuthTries 3
LoginGraceTime 20
ClientAliveInterval 300
ClientAliveCountMax 2
AllowUsers keeper jon
Protocol 2
EOF
sshd -t   # syntax check; refuse to restart if this errors
systemctl restart ssh

Confirm from your second terminal that you can still log in. If you cannot, the original session is your escape hatch.

4. UFW

ufw limit on SSH adds rate-limiting for brute-force at the firewall layer (in addition to fail2ban):

ufw default deny incoming
ufw default allow outgoing
ufw limit 22/tcp comment 'SSH rate-limited'
ufw allow 80/tcp comment 'HTTP (Caddy ACME + redirect)'
ufw allow 443/tcp comment 'HTTPS (Caddy)'
ufw --force enable
ufw status verbose

Caddy needs port 80 open for ACME HTTP-01 challenges and to redirect to HTTPS. No other ports.

5. Caddy install via official apt repo

curl -1sLf 'https://dl.cloudsmith.io/public/caddy/stable/gpg.key' | gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/caddy-stable-archive-keyring.gpg
curl -1sLf 'https://dl.cloudsmith.io/public/caddy/stable/debian.deb.txt' | tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/caddy-stable.list
apt update
apt -y install caddy

The package installs caddy.service, creates the caddy user/group, and starts the service. Confirm:

systemctl status caddy   # active (running)
caddy version            # v2.x.x

6. Caddyfile baseline

Edit /etc/caddy/Caddyfile. The baseline below routes three subdomains, enables JSON access logs (we'll feed those to fail2ban in step 8), and sets sensible timeouts:

{
    email ai@twlv20.com
    log {
        output stderr
        format json
    }
    servers {
        timeouts {
            read_body 30s
            read_header 10s
            write 30s
            idle 5m
        }
    }
}

paperclip.<your-domain> {
    encode zstd gzip
    log {
        output file /var/log/caddy/paperclip-access.log {
            roll_size 50mb
            roll_keep 7
        }
        format json
    }
    reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:3000   # Paperclip default port; List 03 will install
}

claw.<your-domain> {
    encode zstd gzip
    log {
        output file /var/log/caddy/claw-access.log {
            roll_size 50mb
            roll_keep 7
        }
        format json
    }
    reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:4000   # OpenClaw; List 03 installs
}

infisical.<your-domain> {
    encode zstd gzip
    log {
        output file /var/log/caddy/infisical-access.log {
            roll_size 50mb
            roll_keep 7
        }
        format json
    }
    reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:8080   # Infisical; List 02 task 4 installs
}

caddy validate --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
systemctl reload caddy
journalctl -u caddy -n 50 --no-pager   # confirm no errors; confirm cert obtained

Each subdomain will return a 502 until its upstream exists — that's expected. The TLS cert acquisition is the proof point at this stage.

7. fail2ban — SSH jail

cat <<'EOF' > /etc/fail2ban/jail.local
[DEFAULT]
bantime = 1h
findtime = 10m
maxretry = 5
backend = systemd
ignoreip = 127.0.0.1/8 ::1

[sshd]
enabled = true
port = ssh
filter = sshd
maxretry = 3
findtime = 5m
bantime = 24h
EOF
systemctl restart fail2ban
fail2ban-client status sshd

Caddy logs JSON; the simplest approach is a regex filter that matches the JSON status field for repeated 401/403/404 from the same IP. Drop in:

cat <<'EOF' > /etc/fail2ban/filter.d/caddy-status.conf

enabled = true
filter = caddy-status
logpath = /var/log/caddy/*-access.log

findtime = 5m
